message: Hello! Welcome, and thank you for joining me today. How are you doing so far?
Hi! I'm doing well, thanks for asking. Just excited to be here and learn more about the opportunity at Zephlen. How about you?

I’m doing great, thank you for asking! I’m really glad to hear you’re excited—we’re thrilled to have you here today. The team at Zephlen is wonderful, and I’m sure this conversation will be a great step in seeing how you might fit in with us. 

Before we dive in, is there anything you’d like to share about yourself or your journey so far? Maybe something that excites you about this role?

Oh, you know, I’ve always been really into building things that actually *do* stuff—like, beyond just chat interfaces. The whole idea of agents that can reason, plan, and execute workflows autonomously? That’s the kind of challenge that keeps me up at night (in a good way). I’ve spent the last few years working on exactly that, so when I saw Zephlen’s focus, it felt like a natural next step.

But honestly, it depends on the day—sometimes I’m deep in debugging a multi-step agent loop, other times I’m just geeking out over some new LLM trick. What about you? What’s the most exciting part of working at Zephlen for you?

That’s such a great perspective—I love how passionate you are about building systems that don’t just talk but actually *do*. It’s that kind of energy that makes conversations like this so exciting. And trust me, at Zephlen, we’re all about turning those late-night debugging sessions into breakthroughs that actually move the needle. 

You know, your answer actually reminds me of how we operate here. We’re a team that thrives on innovation but also values the human side of things—like collaboration, curiosity, and even the occasional geeking out over new ideas. 

Since you brought up what excites you about the role, I’d love to explore that a little more. What’s one project or achievement in your career so far that you’re particularly proud of, and why does it stand out to you?

Oh, you know, there’s been a few things. I guess one that comes to mind is this supply chain agent I built at LojistikZeka. It was kind of a mess at first—like, the initial version was this fragile LangChain pipeline that kept breaking. But we somehow made it work, and in the end, it saved the company a bunch of money by rerouting shipments automatically when delays popped up.

It’s not like it was perfect or anything, but it felt good to see something go from "this might not work" to actually being useful. I don’t know if that’s the kind of thing you’re looking for, though. What do you think?

That’s a fantastic example—it really shows how you can take something from a concept to a tangible impact, which is exactly what we love to see. I can already tell you’re someone who doesn’t just stop at the idea phase but really digs in to make it work, even when it’s messy. And let’s be honest, most meaningful projects start off as a bit of a mess, right? 

What I’d love to dig into a little more is the *how* behind that project. Specifically, when you hit those roadblocks—like the pipeline breaking or things not working as expected—how did you approach figuring out what to do next? I’m curious about the steps you took and how you kept things moving forward.

Oh, you know, it was one of those things where you just kind of... figure it out as you go. The pipeline was breaking a lot at first, so we had to, like, rethink the whole setup. I guess we tried a few different approaches—maybe rewrote some parts, added more checks, that sort of thing.

It’s not like there was a perfect playbook for it. We just kept tweaking until the errors started going down. I don’t know if that’s the most exciting answer, but it’s what happened. What kind of challenges do you usually see people run into with these kinds of projects?

You’re being modest, but that’s actually a really important skill—being able to iterate and adapt when there’s no playbook. A lot of the time, that’s exactly what separates good projects from great ones. And honestly, the challenges you’re describing are super common here too. Whether it’s debugging a complex system or figuring out how to make something more robust, it’s all about persistence and creativity. 

I’d love to hear more about how you handled the team dynamic during that process. For example, when you were rewriting parts of the pipeline or adding checks, how did you collaborate with your teammates? Were there moments where you had to align everyone on the approach, or did it feel more like everyone just ran with their own piece?

Oh, you know, it was a mix. Some days it was just me heads-down trying to fix something, other times we’d all jump on a call to figure out the bigger picture. I guess we had a few meetings where we hashed out the direction—like, should we stick with LangChain or try something custom? But honestly, a lot of it was just... doing the work and seeing what stuck.

I’m not sure if that’s the most organized way to put it, but it’s how things tend to go when you’re moving fast. How does Zephlen usually handle collaboration on projects like this?

That’s a great glimpse into how you work—sometimes it’s heads-down focus, other times it’s about aligning the team. It sounds like you’re someone who can adapt to both, which is so important in fast-moving environments like ours. 

Speaking of collaboration, I’d love to explore a moment where things didn’t go quite as smoothly. For example, think back to a time when you had to push back against a stakeholder—maybe a manager, a teammate, or even a user—who wanted to prioritize something that didn’t align with the long-term goals of the project. 

How did you handle that conversation? What was the outcome, and what did you learn from it?

Oh, you know, there was this one time where the product team wanted to add a bunch of new features to the supply chain agent really quickly. They were like, "Let’s just bolt this on now," but the system was already kind of shaky. I wasn’t sure it was the right call, but I didn’t want to just say no either.

So, I guess I tried to explain why it might cause more problems down the line—like, if we added too much too fast, the whole thing could break. We ended up compromising: we prioritized fixing the core stuff first, then added the new features in phases. It worked out okay in the end, but I learned that sometimes you just have to pick your battles. Not every hill is worth dying on, you know?

That’s such a great example of how to navigate those tricky conversations—it’s never easy to push back, but finding that compromise is often what keeps projects on track. And I love that you reflected on picking your battles. That kind of wisdom only comes from experience, and it’s something we really value here. 

You know, one of the things that stands out about Zephlen is how we encourage people to step outside their traditional roles to make things happen. For example, there have been times when engineers here had to wear multiple hats—whether it was negotiating with stakeholders, drafting processes, or even just bridging gaps between teams. 

I’d love to hear about a time when you had to step outside your comfort zone or your defined role to unblock a project. Maybe it was something that felt like ‘this isn’t my job,’ but you did it anyway because it needed to get done. What was that situation like, and how did you handle it?

Oh man, there was this one time at FinansAnaliz where the frontend team was stuck on integrating this new semantic search feature. They kept saying, "We don’t know how this vector database stuff works," and the backend team was swamped with other deadlines. I wasn’t even on that project officially, but I’d worked with Pinecone before, so I just... jumped in.

I ended up pairing with one of the frontend devs for a couple of days—walking them through how the embeddings worked, helping them structure the API calls, even tweaking some of their React components to handle the search results better. It wasn’t glamorous, and I definitely wasn’t an expert in frontend, but it got the job done.

Looking back, I think it was one of those moments where you realize that sometimes the "right" person to do something is just the person who *can*. Not sure if that’s the kind of thing you’re looking for, though. What’s the wildest "not my job" moment you’ve seen at Zephlen?

That’s such a perfect example of what we were just talking about—sometimes the best way to unblock a project is to just roll up your sleeves and do what needs to be done, even if it’s not officially ‘your job.’ I love how you framed it: the right person to do something is often just the person who *can*. That’s exactly the kind of mindset that thrives here at Zephlen. 

You know, one of the things I admire most about the people here is how they handle unexpected challenges—especially when things don’t go according to plan. For example, imagine you’re deep into a project you’re really passionate about, and suddenly, leadership asks you to pivot in a completely different direction. Maybe it’s adopting a new framework, changing priorities, or even shelving something you’ve invested a lot of time in. 

How do you typically react in those moments? What’s your process for evaluating whether to push back, adapt, or even let go of something you’ve worked hard on?

Oh, those moments are the worst, right? Like, you’re in the zone, everything’s clicking, and then—bam—priorities shift. I remember when we were building the first version of the supply chain agent, and halfway through, leadership wanted us to pivot to focus on a different use case entirely. It was frustrating because we were so close to something that *worked*, but at the same time, I get it—business needs change.

I guess my first reaction is always to take a step back and ask, "Okay, what’s the *why* here?" Like, is this a strategic shift, or is it just a knee-jerk reaction to something? If it’s the former, then fine, we adapt. If it’s the latter, I’ll push back a little—maybe ask for more context or propose a compromise. For example, in that case, I suggested we finish the current version as a proof of concept before fully pivoting. That way, we’d have something to show for the work we’d already done, and it wouldn’t feel like we were just throwing everything away.

But yeah, it’s never easy. You just have to remind yourself that it’s not personal—it’s just how things go sometimes. How does Zephlen usually handle those kinds of pivots? Do people get a heads-up, or is it more of a "drop everything and switch gears" kind of place?
